Hidden neighbourhood effects may introduce confounding in regression models

Joseph Bulbulia https://josephbulbulia.netlify.app (Victoria University of Wellington)https://www.wgtn.ac.nz
2021-MAY-20

Contents

  • Causal inference when we include neighbourhood effects.
  • Simple causal effects
Show code 
# Set up

# packages
# install_rethinking
# function for installing dependencies
# ipak <- function(pkg){
# new.pkg <- pkg[!(pkg %in% installed.packages()[, "Package"])]
# if (length(new.pkg)) 
#     install.packages(new.pkg, dependencies = TRUE) 
# sapply(pkg, require, character.only = TRUE)
# }
# # usage
# packages <- c("coda", "plyr", "mvtnorm", "scales", "dagitty")
# ipak(packages)
# next install rethinking
if (!require(rethinking)) {
  devtools::install_github("rmcelreath/rethinking")
}
#next install ggdag
if (!require(ggdag)) {
  devtools::install_github("malcolmbarrett/ggdag")
}


# libraries
library("tidyverse")
library("ggdag")
library("brms")
library("rstan")
library("rstanarm")
library("tidybayes")
library("bayesplot")
library("easystats")
# rstan options
rstan_options(auto_write=TRUE)
options(mc.cores=parallel::detectCores ())
theme_set(theme_classic())
Show code 
# options
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = TRUE,
  warning = FALSE,
  message = FALSE,
  layout = "l-body-outset",
  fig.width = 12,
  fig.height = 10,
  collapse = TRUE,
  R.options = list(width = 60)
)

Imagine a word in which we have the following relationships:

Show code 
library(ggdag)
# code for creating a DAG
ggdag_ov <- dagify(Y ~ X + M,
                   M ~ X + U,
                   exposure = "X",
                   outcome = "Y") %>%
  control_for("M")

# plot the DAG
ggdag_ov %>%
  ggdag() + theme_dag_blank()

Suppose that we want to write a mediation model in which we partition out the direct effect of X\(\to\)Y and total effect of X\(\to\)Y through M. However there is an unmeasured neighbourhood effect of U on M. Note that neighbourhood is a collider.

Show code 
# find colliders
ggdag::ggdag_collider( ggdag_ov )  + theme_dag_blank()

Because of this, neighbourhood effects can lead to confounding, making it appear as though the effect of X on Y is negative when we condition on M.

To show this, we simulate some data:

Note that in our simulation, X has a positive effect on Y. However, M is a collider, and including M in our model makes it appear as if X is negatively related to Y, even though in our simulation there is no relationship.

We can write a mediation model that does not adjust for neighbourhood effects (U), imagining these neighbourhood effects are unmeasured (as in our current dataset).

Show code 
bmlm::mlm_path_plot(xlab = "Focal\n(X)",
              mlab = "Mediator\n(M)",
              ylab = "Outcome\n(Y)")

Show code 
path_m <- brms::bf(M ~ X)
path_y <- brms::bf(Y ~ X + M)

m1 <- brms::brm(
  path_m + path_y + set_rescor(FALSE),
  data = d,
  file = here::here("models", "mediation-confound-simulation")
)

# output
# parameters::model_parameters(m1)

For the mediation model we recover the indirect, direct and total effects as follow:

Show code 
# get posterior distributions
post <- brms::posterior_samples(m1)

# sum and multiply the posterior distributions to obtain parameter estimates
post2 <- post %>%
  dplyr::transmute(
    a = b_M_X,
    b = b_Y_M,
    cp = b_Y_X,
    me = a * b,
    c = cp + me#,
    # pme = me / c
  )

The direct effect (cp) appears to be negative!

Show code 
# plot the results
mcmc_intervals(post2)

But recall, we simulated a direct effect coefficient of 0. This seemingly negative effect of X on Y is the result of conditioning on a collider, which opens a path between X and U.

Show code 
ggdag::ggdag_dseparated(ggdag_ov, controlling_for = "M", collider_lines = TRUE )  + theme_dag_blank()

Causal inference when we include neighbourhood effects.

This time, let’s included the previously un-modelled effect of neighbourhoods.

Show code 
path_m <- brms::bf(M ~ X )
path_y <- brms::bf(Y ~ X + M + U)

m2 <- brms::brm(
  path_m + path_y + set_rescor(FALSE),
  data = d,
  file = here::here("models", "mediation-confound-simulation-2")
)

# output
# parameters::model_parameters(m2)

Graphing results in a model that adjusts for neighbourhoods effects we correct for the confounding

Show code 
# get posterior distributions
post2 <- brms::posterior_samples(m2)

# sum and multiply the posterior distributions to obtain parameter estimates
post2.2 <- post2 %>%
  dplyr::transmute(
    a = b_M_X,
    b = b_Y_M,
    cp = b_Y_X,
    me = a * b,
    c = cp + me#,
    # pme = me / c
  )

# plot the results
mcmc_intervals(post2.2)

That is, we recover the simulated direct effect (namely 0)

In short, if we are modelling relationships in which covariates are affected by neighbourhood effects, we might confound our results.

Simple causal effects

The goods news is that for models in which we are only interested in the total effect of X on Y, it is sufficient for us to condition only X. (All backdoor paths are closed)

Show code 
# code for creating a DAG
ggdag_ov2 <- dagify(Y ~ X + M,
                   M ~ X + U,
                   exposure = "X",
                   outcome = "Y")

ggdag::ggdag_adjustment_set(ggdag_ov2)

Doing so gives us X’s total effect, which arises through X’s effect on M.

Show code 
par <- lm( Y ~ X, data = d) 

tab_par <- parameters::model_parameters(par) 

tab_par
Parameter   | Coefficient |   SE |        95% CI | t(98) |     p
----------------------------------------------------------------
(Intercept) |       -0.03 | 0.40 | [-0.83, 0.77] | -0.08 | 0.937
X           |        1.30 | 0.43 | [ 0.44, 2.16] |  3.01 | 0.003
Show code 
tab_par %>%
  plot()

However, in such a model, the mechanism for the total effect of X on Y would remain obscure. And we should not be misled into thinking that X directly causes Y, even if this model contains no other covariates.
